plainMeaning: I now resolve to praise all twenty-four supreme teachers — the ones who light up the world, build the path to liberation, and know everything.
recitation: logassa ujjoagare, dhamma-titthayare jiṇe; arihaṁte kittaïssaṁ, caüvvīsaṁ pi kevalī.

wordByWord.gloss: of the world / of the cosmos
wordByWord.etymology: Genitive sg. of Ardhamāgadhī loga (< Sanskrit loka, 'world, universe'); the -assa ending is the Prakrit genitive.
wordByWord.notes: Governs ujjoagare: 'illuminators of the world'. The whole hymn takes its popular name from this opening word.

wordByWord.etymology: Compound ujjoa (< Sanskrit udyota, 'light, illumination') + gara/kara ('maker, doer'); accusative plural agreeing with jiṇe and arihaṁte.
wordByWord.notes: Figures kevala-jñāna (omniscience) as a light that lights up reality for all beings.

wordByWord.gloss: of the dharma / of the path
wordByWord.etymology: Ardhamāgadhī dhamma < Sanskrit dharma ('the teaching, the right path, moral order').
wordByWord.notes: First member of the compound dhamma-titthayare; here the 'ford' that the Jinas make is the dharma itself.

wordByWord.etymology: tittha (< Sanskrit tīrtha, 'ford, crossing-place') + yara/kara ('maker'); accusative plural. The whole dhamma-titthayare = 'makers of the dharma-ford'.
wordByWord.notes: Title term glossed in the preface; here it is built into a compound with dhamma and used as an accusative object of praise, not a bare epithet.

wordByWord.etymology: Ardhamāgadhī jiṇa < Sanskrit jina ('conqueror', from √ji, 'to conquer'); accusative plural -e.
wordByWord.notes: Conquerors of the inner passions (rāga-dveṣa); see preface glossary.

wordByWord.etymology: ari ('foe') + hanta ('slayer') → 'destroyer of inner foes'; alternatively arha ('worthy') + the honorific, 'the worthy ones'. Accusative plural -e.
wordByWord.notes: Direct object of kittaïssaṁ: 'I will praise the Arihantas'. Living, fully-awakened Tīrthaṅkaras.

wordByWord.gloss: I will praise / I shall extol
wordByWord.etymology: First-person singular future of kitt- (< Sanskrit √kīrt, 'to praise, celebrate'); -issaṁ is the Prakrit future ending. Cf. preface term kīrtana.
wordByWord.notes: The pivotal word: a future-tense vow of intention (saṅkalpa) that frames the entire hymn — 'I will praise', setting up the roll-call of vv.2–4.

wordByWord.etymology: caüvvīsaṁ (< Sanskrit caturviṁśati, 'twenty-four'), accusative, + the emphatic particle pi (< Sanskrit api, 'even, also').
wordByWord.notes: 100% coverage: the printed token fuses the numeral with the particle pi ('even/all of them'), stressing that not one of the twenty-four is left unpraised. Spelt with doubled -vv- here (caüvvīsaṁpi), versus single -v- caüvīsaṁpi in v.5 — a deliberate book variant, preserved verbatim, not normalised.

wordByWord.etymology: kevala ('sole, absolute, complete' → kevala-jñāna, omniscience) + possessive -in/-ī ('one who has'); nominative/accusative plural form kevalī.
wordByWord.notes: Closing epithet of the verse; every Tīrthaṅkara is a kevalin. See preface glossary.

literalTranslation: The illuminators of the world, the makers of the dharma-ford, the Jinas, the Arihantas — I will praise all twenty-four of them, the omniscient ones.
idiomaticTranslation: I now resolve to sing the praise of the Arihantas — those who flood the world with light, who carve out the ford of dharma across the ocean of rebirth, the Victors, the all-knowing ones: all twenty-four, every last one of them.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This opening verse is pure resolve. Its grammatical and devotional centre is one future-tense verb, kittaïssaṁ — 'I will praise' — which turns the hymn into a vow of intention (saṅkalpa) before a single Tīrthaṅkara is named. Everything else in the verse is the accusative object of that one verb: the reciter heaps up five descriptors of the twenty-four, all in the plural, before committing to praise them. The ordering of the epithets is a deliberate ascent. First the Jinas are 'illuminators of the world' (logassa ujjoagare) — known by their public effect, light for all beings. Then 'makers of the dharma-ford' (dhamma-titthayare) — known by their work, building the crossing over saṁsāra. Then 'Jinas' (jiṇe) — known by their inner victory over the passions. Then 'Arihantas' (arihaṁte) — the worthy, awakened, living teachers. Finally 'all twenty-four... the omniscient ones' (caüvvīsaṁ pi kevalī) — known by their attainment, kevala-jñāna. The little particle pi ('even, all') does quiet but crucial work: it forecloses any partiality, insisting that every one of the twenty-four, without exception, is included in the coming praise. The verse thus moves from the cosmic (light for the world) to the personal (a named, complete roster), and from outward radiance to inner omniscience — and then stops, deliberately, at the threshold of the naming that vv.2–4 will carry out.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse compresses several distinct titles of a perfected being and lets the reciter feel that they coincide in one person: ujjoagare (illuminator) names the function of omniscience; titthayara (ford-maker) names the salvific role of founding the fourfold community and the path; jina (victor) names the conquest of rāga and dveṣa; arihanta names the destruction of the four ghātī (destroying) karmas; kevalin names the resulting kevala-jñāna. Jain doctrine holds these as facets of a single state — every Tīrthaṅkara is a Jina, an Arihanta and a Kevalin — and the verse's piling-up of epithets is a confession of exactly that identity. The future tense (kittaïssaṁ) is itself doctrinally apt: praise (kīrtana) is undertaken as a vow of the will, an act of bhāva (inner intention) that purifies the reciter, not a favour to the already-liberated who need nothing.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: When you recite this verse, you are not yet naming anyone — you are forming the intention. Let the first gāthā be the moment you gather the mind and consciously resolve, 'I will praise', so that the twenty-four names that follow are spoken from settled intention rather than rote. The word pi ('all of them') is a useful check: hold the whole company of twenty-four in view at once before you begin to count them one by one.

crossReferences.text: Digambara parallel 'Thossāmi' (Tirthaṅkara-bhakti), which opens the same hymn with an additional verse
crossReferences.source: Y. K. Malaiya, after Ācārya Tulsī, Sacitra Śrāvaka Pratikramaṇa

sources.title: Chatur-Vinshati Stava (Logassa Sutra), with section-by-section translation (after Ācārya Tulsī)
sources.consultedFor: Verbatim Prakrit text and literal English of v.1 (ujjoagare 'enlightened the world', dhamma-titthayara, 'I praise the Arhantas, the 24 kevalis').

sources.title: Logassa — meaning and explanation (JVB Houston)
sources.consultedFor: Independent confirmation of the line-by-line sense of v.1 and of kittaïssaṁ as 'I will praise'.

sources.title: Logassa Sootra — Pathshala commentary
sources.consultedFor: Devotional gloss of the five epithets (illuminator, ford-maker, Jina, Arihanta, Kevalin) in v.1.

sources.title: Line-by-line meaning of Logassa Sutra (JainKnowledge / JainGPT Q&A)
sources.consultedFor: Cross-check of 'arihante kittaissam chauvisam pi kevali' = 'I will praise the Arihants, all twenty-four Kevalis'.

notes: Token verbatim from the Anchalgachchha book: caüvvīsaṁpi is printed with doubled -vv- in v.1 but single -v- (caüvīsaṁpi) in v.5; both preserved, not normalised (per §7). The token ચઉવ્વીસંપિ fuses the numeral caüvvīsaṁ with the emphatic particle pi (api). dhamma and titthayare are printed as separate words but function as one compound, dhamma-titthayare ('maker of the dharma-ford'). Some published recensions read 'arahante' for the book's 'arihaṁte', and place a daṇḍa differently; the gloss follows this edition's orthography. Confidence is high on the standard meaning of every token; the only minor uncertainty is whether kevalī here is best parsed as accusative plural in apposition (followed here) or as a loosely-appended honorific.
